title: [E] close() does not drain in-flight write queue before releasing resources
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts (TRAGarPersistentInstance.close())
**Scenario**: If close() is called while an ingest() call is awaiting #appendChunks (i.e. the write is in-flight), close() can release the backend handle before the write completes. The ingest() call may then fail with a raw backend error rather than a clean TRAGarError.
**Risk**: Partially-written store on concurrent close+ingest, raw untyped errors surfaced to caller.
**What to do**: close() should await the write queue drain before marking the instance closed and releasing the backend.

title: [B/E] paragraphChunk() has no max-chunk-size guard — long paragraphs silently truncated by embedder
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:66-71
**Issue**: paragraphChunk() splits on blank lines only. A document with no blank lines (or a single giant paragraph) becomes one chunk silently passed to the embedder. The embedder will silently truncate it at its token limit (typically 512 tokens for MiniLM). No warning, no error, no indication to the caller.
**Risk**: Silent information loss on long documents. The most important content at the end of a long paragraph is dropped without any observable signal.
**What to do**: Add a max-chunk-token-or-character limit to paragraphChunk(); emit a DequantizationRequested-style warning when a chunk is truncated, or split long paragraphs further.

title: [D] spec requires ModelMismatch error on embedder model conflict; not implemented
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §TRAGar Factory — Scenario: Model mismatch on open: 'WHEN TRAGar.create({ embedder: customEmbedder }) is called against a namespace that stores a different modelId THEN create() rejects with a ModelMismatch error'; error-model/spec.md lists ModelMismatch in ErrorCode table
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:458-499 (#loadFromStore) — reads meta.json but never compares meta.modelId against the configured embedder's modelId; no ModelMismatch error code in types.ts
**Mismatch**: Spec requires model mismatch detection and rejection. Code reads modelId from store but never checks it against the embedder config.
**Why it matters**: Silently mixing embeddings from different models corrupts similarity scores without warning.
**What to do**: After loading meta.json, if embedder is provided and meta.modelId != embedder.modelId, throw TRAGarError('ModelMismatch', ...); add ModelMismatch to ErrorCode.

title: [D] TRAGar.create() requires store+embedder config — spec allows zero-argument call
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §TRAGar Factory — 'All five seams (chunker, embedder, store, index, reranker) SHALL have working defaults when opts is omitted'; Scenario: Zero-configuration creation: 'WHEN TRAGar.create() is called with no arguments THEN a TRAGar instance is returned with namespace = default and all five default seams active'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:768 — create(config: CreateConfig) — config is required; CreateConfig.store is required (no default); CreateConfig.embedder is optional
**Mismatch**: Spec says create() with no args must work. Code requires a CreateConfig object with a required store property. Calling TRAGar.create() with no arguments will throw a TypeError at runtime.
**Why it matters**: Zero-config creation is a primary UX requirement and DX selling point; currently broken.
**What to do**: Make config optional in create(); default store to stores.opfs() and embedder to embedders.transformers().

title: [A] loadPipeline() race condition: concurrent embed() calls can trigger multiple pipeline loads
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:34-48
**Issue**: loadPipeline() checks 'if (pipeline !== null) return pipeline' then awaits import('@xenova/transformers') and the pipeline constructor. If two concurrent embed() calls arrive before the first load completes, both see pipeline === null and both proceed to load the model. The second load overwrites pipeline with a second instance. The model is loaded twice (network + memory overhead), and the first pipeline instance is leaked.
**Why it matters**: Double model load is expensive (~23 MB for the default model). In environments with connection limits, concurrent ingest() calls at startup will race and potentially cause an EmbedderLoadFailed if the module is slow or fails on the second concurrent attempt.
**What to do**: Replace the null-check pattern with a promise cache: store the in-flight loadPipeline() promise itself so subsequent calls await the same promise.

title: [D] transformers embedder throws EmbedderLoadFailed but spec and error-model require EmbedderUnavailable
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: embedder-transformers/spec.md §Embedder Error Model — Scenario: Network failure during load: 'embed() rejects with an error whose code === EmbedderUnavailable'; error-model/spec.md §ErrorCode Enumeration lists EmbedderUnavailable not EmbedderLoadFailed
**Code**: js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:41-48 — throws TRAGarError('EmbedderLoadFailed', ...); js/types.ts:13 — ErrorCode includes 'EmbedderLoadFailed'
**Mismatch**: Spec defines the code as EmbedderUnavailable; code uses EmbedderLoadFailed. These are different string literals.
**Why it matters**: Any caller catching by code === 'EmbedderUnavailable' (following the spec) will miss the error.
**What to do**: Rename 'EmbedderLoadFailed' → 'EmbedderUnavailable' in types.ts ErrorCode and in transformers-embedder.ts throw site.

title: [A] #appendChunks reads full chunks.jsonl on every ingest call — O(n) reads
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:514-535
**Issue**: TRAGarPersistentInstance.#appendChunks() reads the entire existing chunks.jsonl from the backend on every ingest() call (line 515: this.#backend.read('chunks.jsonl')), then concatenates the new lines and writes the full file back. For a corpus with N existing chunks and B new chunks, this is O(N+B) data transferred on every ingest, not O(B). As the corpus grows, ingest performance degrades linearly.
**Why it matters**: Ingesting large corpora incrementally becomes increasingly slow. A corpus with 10,000 chunks means transferring ~10,000 lines from storage on every single-document ingest.
**What to do**: Use an append-only write strategy: maintain an offset pointer into the file or use the FileBackend.write semantics with an 'append' mode. Alternatively, add an append() method to the FileBackend interface.

title: [D] store-opfs spec requires 6-file layout; code only writes meta.json and chunks.jsonl
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: store-opfs/spec.md §OPFS Filesystem Layout — directory MUST contain exactly: meta.json, vectors.bin, scales.bin, chunks.jsonl, lookup.bin, tombstones.bin
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:458-499 (#loadFromStore reads only meta.json and chunks.jsonl); js/tragar.ts:514-536 (#appendChunks writes only chunks.jsonl and meta.json); opfs-store.ts writes no binary files
**Mismatch**: Spec mandates 6 files including binary vector/scale/lookup/tombstone files. Code writes only 2 JSON/JSONL files and stores vectors inline in chunks.jsonl as JSON arrays.
**Why it matters**: Binary file layout is required for 4× storage efficiency and C++ SIMD index compatibility; current format is incompatible with the spec's append-only semantics and compact() design.
**What to do**: File spec/code divergence; this is intentional for the JS-only slice — needs a spec note or separate file-layout spec for the JS persistence layer.

title: [D] onWarn receives (code, message) strings but spec defines typed Warning objects
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Warning Callback — 'callback SHALL receive typed Warning objects covering: OversizeChunk, LongChunk, QuantizationLossHigh, DegenerateVector, and StoreFallback' with fields like { kind: 'OversizeChunk', source, line, tokenCount }
**Code**: js/types.ts:148 (onWarn?: (code: WarnCode, message: string) => void); all onWarn calls pass (WarnCode, string)
**Mismatch**: Spec requires structured Warning objects with kind + typed fields. Code uses a flat (code: WarnCode, message: string) callback signature with no structured payload.
**Why it matters**: Callers cannot programmatically handle specific warning conditions (e.g., log OversizeChunk with its token count) without parsing the message string.
**What to do**: Define a Warning union type with discriminated fields per kind; update onWarn signature to (warning: Warning) => void; update all emission sites.

title: [D] TRAGarError missing required cause field from error-model spec
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: error-model/spec.md §TRAGarError Base Class — 'TRAGarError MUST expose: cause?: unknown — the underlying platform error (DOMException, etc.) when wrapping a lower-level failure'
**Code**: js/errors.ts:6-14 — TRAGarError constructor takes only (code, message); no cause parameter or property
**Mismatch**: Spec requires a cause?: unknown field for error chaining from platform errors (DOMException, etc.). The class has no such field.
**Why it matters**: Error chain is lost when OPFS/IDB errors are wrapped; impossible to debug platform-level failures.
**What to do**: Add cause?: unknown parameter to TRAGarError constructor; store as readonly property; pass cause when throwing StoreUnavailable from opfs-store.ts and fs-store.ts.

title: [E] Transformers.js embed() does not guard against out-of-memory on large batches
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:55-64
**Scenario**: embed(batch) passes the entire batch array directly to the pipeline in one call with no size limit. A very large batch (e.g. a document chunked into thousands of paragraphs) will attempt to allocate a single Float32Array for all vectors at once inside the WASM heap. An OOM inside the WASM runtime throws a RuntimeError ('OOM' or 'out of memory') which is not caught by the try/catch in loadPipeline — it propagates as a raw RuntimeError, not a TRAGarError.
**Risk**: OOM errors escape as raw RuntimeError, bypassing all TRAGarError handling in calling code. No batch-size guidance exists in the public API.
**What to do**: Catch RuntimeError and similar errors in embed() and re-throw as TRAGarError('EmbedderError', ...); document recommended batch size limits.

title: [D] ErrorCode set diverges from error-model spec — 7 codes missing, 4 not in spec
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: error-model/spec.md §ErrorCode Enumeration — specifies exactly 12 codes: StoreUnavailable, StoreCorrupt, SchemaTooNew, SchemaTooOld, ModelMismatch, EmbedderUnavailable, EmbedderInputTooLong, EmbedderRuntimeError, QuotaExceeded, NamespaceLocked, InvalidConfig, Internal
**Code**: js/types.ts:12-20 — defines 8 codes: InstanceClosed, InvalidConfig, EmbedderLoadFailed, SchemaTooNew, SchemaTooOld, NamespaceLocked, NotFound, StoreUnavailable
**Mismatch**: Code has InstanceClosed (not in spec), EmbedderLoadFailed (spec says EmbedderUnavailable), NotFound (not in spec). Code is missing: StoreCorrupt, ModelMismatch, EmbedderUnavailable, EmbedderInputTooLong, EmbedderRuntimeError, QuotaExceeded, Internal. 7 spec-required codes do not exist; 3 code-only codes have no spec coverage.
**Why it matters**: Any caller catching errors by code will find the code string wrong or missing for most error conditions.
**What to do**: Align ErrorCode union in types.ts with error-model spec; rename EmbedderLoadFailed→EmbedderUnavailable; add missing codes; decide fate of InstanceClosed and NotFound.

title: [E] ingest() with empty doc.source produces bad chunk IDs and filenames
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:54-61 (chunkId), js/tragar.ts:228-243 (ingest)
**Scenario**: doc.source is not validated before use. An empty string ('') is accepted; chunkId('', 0, text) produces a valid but non-unique-per-document hash (two different documents with empty source and same paragraph text produce identical chunk IDs). In the fs-store, source is not used as a filename, so no I/O error surfaces.
**Risk**: Duplicate chunk IDs cause silent data corruption: multiple chunks share an ID, getVector(id) returns only the first match, and export/import round-trips may deduplicate or overwrite records.
**What to do**: Validate doc.source in ingest() — require it to be a non-empty string; throw TRAGarError('InvalidInput', ...) if blank.

title: [D] Hit type missing line and meta fields required by spec
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Query Methods Basic query scenario — 'each with id, score, text, source, line, and meta'
**Code**: js/types.ts:109-118 (Hit interface has chunkId, text, source, score — no line, no meta)
**Mismatch**: Spec says Hit must include line (1-indexed source line) and meta (caller-supplied metadata passthrough). Neither field exists in the Hit type.
**Why it matters**: Callers cannot determine where in the source file a chunk originated, and cannot retrieve doc-level metadata from results.
**What to do**: Add line: number and meta?: Record<string, unknown> to the Hit interface; populate them in both query() implementations and in ingest() when building StoredChunk.

title: [E] meta.json written with stale count when #appendChunks write fails mid-way
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:514-536 (#appendChunks)
**Scenario**: #appendChunks writes chunks.jsonl first (line 534), then calls #writeMeta() (line 535). If the process/tab is killed between these two writes, or if #writeMeta() throws (e.g. quota hit on the second write), meta.json reflects the old count while chunks.jsonl already contains the new lines.
**Risk**: On next open(), meta.count is lower than the actual number of lines in chunks.jsonl. While load parses all lines regardless of meta.count today, any future code that trusts meta.count will see an inconsistency, and the file is in a torn state with no detection mechanism.
**What to do**: Write meta.json atomically after chunks, and/or add a checksum/line-count field to meta that is validated during #loadFromStore(). Document the torn-write risk.

title: [D] query() filter option absent from QueryOptions and implementation
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Query Methods — 'An optional filter narrows the candidate set before scoring'; Scenario: Source filter — query(text, { filter: { source: 'readme.md' } })
**Code**: js/types.ts:103-106 (QueryOptions has only k?: number), js/tragar.ts:245 and 559 (query uses only opts?.k)
**Mismatch**: Spec defines a filter field for QueryOptions; it is completely absent from the type and both query() implementations.
**Why it matters**: Source-scoped retrieval is a documented public API scenario; feature is entirely missing.
**What to do**: Add filter: Filter type to QueryOptions; implement pre-scoring filter in both query() and queryStream() methods.

title: [D] delete(), compact(), clear() maintenance methods entirely missing
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Maintenance Methods — 'delete(filter) — soft-deletes chunks; compact() — rewrites store removing tombstones; clear() — removes all chunks'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts, js/types.ts — TRAGarInstance interface and both classes define none of these three methods
**Mismatch**: Spec requires three maintenance methods; none exist in any layer.
**Why it matters**: Without delete/compact/clear, corpus data can never be removed; namespace management is impossible.
**What to do**: Add delete(), compact(), clear() to TRAGarInstance interface and both memory and persistent implementations.

title: [E] export('json') produces wrong vectors_b64 when dim=0 and corpus is empty
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:341-350 (MemoryInstance export), js/tragar.ts:652-663 (PersistentInstance export)
**Scenario**: export('json') computes dim = this.dim || (this.#chunks[0]?.vector.length ?? 0). If dim is 0 (no embedder configured or embedder dim not set) and the corpus is empty, dim stays 0. new Float32Array(0 * 0) = Float32Array(0), which is fine. But if dim=0 and chunks exist (possible when embedder.dim was not set correctly), vectorData is Float32Array(count * 0) = zero-length, and the base64 blob encodes nothing while meta.count > 0 — a silently invalid export.
**Risk**: Exported JSON file is structurally invalid (count > 0 but empty vectors_b64). Any importer will reconstruct zero vectors, silently losing all embeddings.
**What to do**: Before building the export, assert dim > 0 or derive it from chunks[0].vector.length; throw TRAGarError('InvalidState', ...) if it cannot be determined.

title: [D] duplicate chunk suppression not implemented in ingest()
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Ingestion Methods — 'Duplicate chunks (matched by SHA-256 hash) MUST be silently skipped and counted in newChunkCount'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:228-243 (TRAGarMemoryInstance.ingest), js/tragar.ts:538-557 (TRAGarPersistentInstance.ingest) — both push every chunk unconditionally with no dedup check
**Mismatch**: Spec requires deduplication by SHA-256 hash; code appends all chunks without any duplicate check.
**Why it matters**: Re-ingesting a document silently doubles the corpus, corrupting retrieval quality and inflating storage.
**What to do**: Before pushing a new chunk, check whether its id already exists in #chunks (memory) or chunks.jsonl (persistent); skip if found.

title: [D] ingestMany() method entirely missing from implementation
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Ingestion Methods — 'ingestMany(docs) for batched ingestion … returns a settled array (IngestManyResult[]) where each entry is {status: fulfilled, value: IngestResult} or {status: rejected, reason: TRAGarError}'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts (no ingestMany anywhere), js/types.ts:170 (TRAGarInstance interface has no ingestMany)
**Mismatch**: Spec requires ingestMany() with partial-failure semantics. Neither the interface nor either instance class defines it.
**Why it matters**: Batch ingest with fault isolation is a primary use-case for the public API; entire feature is absent.
**What to do**: Add ingestMany() to TRAGarInstance interface and both implementations.

title: [D] ingest() returns void but spec requires IngestResult
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Ingestion Methods — 'Both MUST return an IngestResult that includes the source, total chunk count, new (non-duplicate) chunk count, and elapsed milliseconds'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:228 (async ingest(doc: IngestDoc): Promise<void>), js/types.ts:170 (ingest(doc: IngestDoc): Promise<void>)
**Mismatch**: Spec mandates return of IngestResult with source, chunkCount, newChunkCount, elapsedMs. Both the interface and implementation return Promise<void>.
**Why it matters**: Callers cannot get duplicate-suppression counts or timing data without IngestResult; spec contract is completely unimplemented.
**What to do**: Add IngestResult type to types.ts, update TRAGarInstance interface, update both instance implementations.

title: [D] close() rejects InstanceClosed but spec says StoreUnavailable
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Instance Lifecycle — 'After close(), all further method calls MUST reject with StoreUnavailable'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:214-216, 367-371, 442-444, 679-683 (#assertOpen throws InstanceClosed)
**Mismatch**: Spec says post-close rejections use code 'StoreUnavailable'. Code throws TRAGarError('InstanceClosed', ...) on every method after close().
**Why it matters**: Callers catching errors by code will use the wrong predicate; breaking contract diverges from spec.
**What to do**: Update spec or code to agree. Spec §Lifecycle says StoreUnavailable; type.ts ErrorCode includes InstanceClosed. Decide canonical code and apply consistently.

title: [E] IndexedDB fallback failure throws raw IDBRequest error, not TRAGarError
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/opfs-store.ts:103-113 (openIndexedDb), 78-95 (IdbFileBackend.write)
**Scenario**: When IndexedDB is also unavailable (private browsing in Firefox, storage blocked by policy), req.onerror fires and the Promise rejects with req.error — a raw DOMException, not a TRAGarError. Similarly, IdbFileBackend.write() rejects with req.error directly on failure (line 93).
**Risk**: Callers receive a DOMException instead of TRAGarError('StoreUnavailable', ...). Code paths that catch instanceof TRAGarError will miss it; the error bubbles out of create() unwrapped.
**What to do**: In openIndexedDb and IdbFileBackend.read/write, convert the DOMException rejection to throw new TRAGarError('StoreUnavailable', req.error?.message ?? 'IndexedDB error').

title: [E] Embedder batch returns wrong vector count — unchecked
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:235-242 and js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:55-64
**Scenario**: ingest() calls embedder.embed(paragraphs) and assumes vectors[i] exists for every i < paragraphs.length. If a custom embedder or a future model returns a batch with fewer vectors than inputs (e.g. it silently drops empty strings), vectors[i] is undefined and new Float32Array(undefined) throws a TypeError, not a TRAGarError.
**Risk**: Uncaught TypeError bubbles out of ingest() with no structured error code, breaking callers that check for TRAGarError. Also, the transformers.js path uses out.dims[1] without checking that dims has at least two elements, which would throw if dims is unexpectedly short.
**What to do**: After embed() returns, assert vectors.length === paragraphs.length and that each vector has the expected dimensionality; throw TRAGarError('EmbedderError', ...) if not.

title: [E] k=0 or negative k returns wrong number of results
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:251 and 565 (k = opts?.k ?? 10)
**Scenario**: If a caller passes opts.k = 0, scored.slice(0, 0) returns an empty array — silent, no error. If opts.k is negative (e.g. -1), Array.slice(0, -1) returns all elements except the last, which is a completely wrong behaviour for 'give me the top -1 results'.
**Risk**: Negative k silently returns nearly all chunks; callers expecting an error get a large unexpected result set. k=0 looks like 'no results found' rather than a config error.
**What to do**: Validate opts.k at the top of query() and queryStream(): if defined and not a positive integer, throw TRAGarError('InvalidConfig', ...).

title: [E] query() with empty string produces misleading results
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:245-265 (MemoryInstance), js/tragar.ts:559-578 (PersistentInstance)
**Scenario**: query('') is not rejected. An empty string is passed to embedder.embed(['']). For the transformers.js embedder this may return a zero or near-zero vector; after l2Normalize, a zero-length vector is left unchanged (the norm===0 branch skips division). The dot products will all be ~0 and the ranking is arbitrary/undefined.
**Risk**: Silent garbage results — callers receive k hits ordered by numerical noise rather than semantic relevance. No error is thrown and there is no indication the results are meaningless.
**What to do**: Validate that the query string is non-empty (after trim) at the top of query() and queryStream(), throw TRAGarError('InvalidInput', ...) if blank.

title: [A] fs-store read() returns Uint8Array sharing Buffer's backing ArrayBuffer
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/fs-store.ts:29-30
**Issue**: fs.readFile() in Node.js returns a Buffer. The code wraps it as new Uint8Array(buf.buffer, buf.byteOffset, buf.byteLength). Node.js Buffers can share a pooled ArrayBuffer (Buffer.allocUnsafe pool), meaning buf.buffer may be much larger than the file, and the returned Uint8Array view starts at byteOffset into that shared buffer. Callers that do new Float32Array(result.buffer) will interpret the entire pool ArrayBuffer, not just the file data.
**Why it matters**: When #loadFromStore passes the raw bytes to new TextDecoder().decode(), it works correctly (TextDecoder respects the view bounds). But if any code path does new Float32Array(chunksBytes.buffer) it silently reads garbage from the pool.
**What to do**: Use buf.slice() or Buffer.from(buf) to get an isolated copy, then wrap: new Uint8Array(buf.slice().buffer).

title: [E] Corrupt chunks.jsonl line silently skipped during load
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:487-497
**Scenario**: #loadFromStore() iterates over lines in chunks.jsonl and calls JSON.parse(trimmed) with no try/catch. If a single line is malformed (partial write, truncation, encoding error), the entire open() call throws an unguarded SyntaxError that is not wrapped in TRAGarError.
**Risk**: A single corrupted record — possible after a browser crash mid-write — makes the entire namespace inaccessible with a raw SyntaxError instead of a recoverable TRAGarError, giving callers no structured way to detect or recover from partial corruption.
**What to do**: Wrap the JSON.parse in try/catch; either throw TRAGarError('StorageCorrupted', ...) or emit onWarn and skip the bad line (document the trade-off).

title: [E] StorageQuotaExceeded swallowed as generic StoreUnavailable
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/opfs-store.ts:51-57 and js/seams/fs-store.ts:36-44
**Scenario**: In OpfsFileBackend.write(), a QuotaExceededError thrown by w.write() propagates up uncaught (no try/catch around the write body). In NodeFsFileBackend.write(), any fs error is caught and re-thrown as TRAGarError('StoreUnavailable', msg) without distinguishing quota vs permission vs disk-full.
**Risk**: Callers cannot distinguish a quota error (user must clear storage) from a transient I/O error (safe to retry) — they receive the same opaque error code.
**What to do**: Catch quota/ENOSPC/EDQUOT errors and re-throw as TRAGarError('QuotaExceeded', ...) so callers can surface actionable guidance.

title: [E] OPFS write leaves writable stream open on error
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/opfs-store.ts:51-57
**Scenario**: OpfsFileBackend.write() calls fh.createWritable() then w.write(). If w.write() throws (e.g. QuotaExceededError, device error), the code falls out of the function without calling w.close() or w.abort(). The writable stream handle remains open.
**Risk**: An unclosed FileSystemWritableFileStream holds an exclusive write lock on the file. Subsequent writes to the same path will fail or block until the page is reloaded.
**What to do**: Wrap the write in a try/finally block that calls w.abort() on error and w.close() on success, ensuring the stream is always released.

title: [A] TRAGarPersistentInstance.close() does not flush in-flight writes
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:678
**Issue**: close() sets #closed = true immediately and resolves without waiting for or cancelling any concurrent ingest() calls. Because ingest() calls #appendChunks() which performs two backend writes (chunks.jsonl then meta.json), a race between close() and ingest() can leave the store with an updated chunks.jsonl but a stale meta.json (count mismatch), or with a partially written file.
**Why it matters**: Persistent store corruption on close; the meta count will not match the actual JSONL line count, causing incorrect stats and potential re-load failures.
**What to do**: Track in-flight async operations and either await them in close(), or use a mutex/lock pattern to prevent new writes after close() is called.

title: [A] JSON.parse of persisted data is unguarded — no schema validation
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:467 (#loadFromStore)
**Issue**: meta.json and chunks.jsonl are parsed with JSON.parse and cast with 'as Meta' and 'as ChunkRecord' respectively, with no runtime validation of the resulting shape. If either file is corrupt, partially written, or tampered with, accessing missing fields (e.g. meta.createdAt, rec.vector) either returns undefined silently or throws a TypeError that is not wrapped in a TRAGarError.
**Why it matters**: Corrupt persisted stores will surface as confusing TypeErrors rather than actionable TRAGarError codes. Missing vector field on ChunkRecord causes Float32Array(undefined) downstream.
**What to do**: Add minimal shape guards for the parsed objects and throw TRAGarError('SchemaTooOld' or a new 'CorruptStore' code) when required fields are absent.

title: [E] ingest() memory partially updated on storage write failure
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:554-556
**Scenario**: In TRAGarPersistentInstance.ingest(), #appendChunks is awaited first (line 555). If the write succeeds but a subsequent error is thrown (or if the order is reversed and the write fails), the in-memory #chunks array is updated while storage is not — or vice versa. Currently chunks are pushed to memory after the write, so a write failure leaves memory inconsistent with what was already computed but nothing persisted.
**Risk**: After a storage error, the instance is in an undefined partially-updated state; future queries return stale results and further writes compound the inconsistency.
**What to do**: Stage newChunks to memory only after confirming storage write succeeded, and document that ingest() is not atomic across the memory+storage boundary.

title: [E] Embedder pipeline loaded twice on concurrent first calls
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:34-48
**Scenario**: Two embed() calls arrive before pipeline is set (pipeline === null). Both pass the null check concurrently, both call import('@xenova/transformers') and p('feature-extraction', modelId), downloading and initialising the model twice.
**Risk**: Race causes duplicate model downloads, double memory allocation (~46 MB for default model), and non-deterministic which pipeline survives in the closure.
**What to do**: Replace the null-check guard with a promise-based lock: on first call, store a Promise<EmbedPipeline> in a 'loading' variable; subsequent concurrent callers await that same promise.
close_reason: Duplicate of tRAG-7jj — same root cause (loadPipeline promise-cache race), same fix

title: [A] embed() return type not validated — dim mismatch undetected
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:238, js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:59
**Issue**: embed() is declared to return Promise<Float32Array[]>, but neither the memory nor persistent instance validates that the dimension of returned vectors matches the configured this.dim. If the transformer pipeline returns vectors of the wrong dimension (e.g. after a model mismatch), dotProduct() silently computes a partial or incorrect score.
**Why it matters**: Dimension mismatches corrupt search results without any error signal. In the transformers seam, actualDim is read from out.dims[1] which may be undefined (no bounds check), yielding NaN slices.
**What to do**: Validate returned vector dimensions against this.dim (when dim > 0) after embed() returns; throw TRAGarError('InvalidConfig', ...) on mismatch. Also guard out.dims[1] in transformers-embedder.ts.

title: [E] No mutex on concurrent ingest() calls
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:538-556
**Scenario**: Two concurrent ingest() calls on the same persistent instance both call #appendChunks. Both read the current chunks.jsonl, then both write back a combined version — the second write silently overwrites the first, losing whichever batch committed first.
**Risk**: Data loss on concurrent ingestion. Both calls complete without error but one batch disappears from storage.
**What to do**: Add a per-instance async mutex (e.g. a promise chain or a lock flag) that serialises #appendChunks calls.

title: [A] vectors[i] access without bounds check in ingest()
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:238 and js/tragar.ts:549
**Issue**: In both TRAGarMemoryInstance.ingest() and TRAGarPersistentInstance.ingest(), the code accesses vectors[i] (the result of embedder.embed()) without checking that the returned array has at least paragraphs.length entries. If the embedder returns fewer vectors than input paragraphs, this silently produces undefined, and new Float32Array(undefined) throws a TypeError that is not wrapped in a TRAGarError.
**Why it matters**: Any custom embedder that returns the wrong number of vectors will produce an uncaught TypeError instead of a TRAGarError, breaking the consistent error-handling contract and leaking implementation details.
**What to do**: After calling embedder.embed(), assert vectors.length === paragraphs.length and throw TRAGarError('InvalidConfig', ...) if the count mismatches.

title: QA Round: Code, Docs, Spec, Tests, Robustness
description: Comprehensive QA sweep across six domains: code quality, documentation quality, spec-vs-implementation mismatches, missing specs, test coverage, test quality, and code robustness. This epic tracks ~100 findings generated by parallel domain analysis agents. No implementation — findings only.

title: QA Round v0.1: Comprehensive quality audit
description: Full-sweep quality audit across seven domains: code quality, documentation quality, spec↔code alignment, missing specs, test coverage, test quality, and code robustness. Target: ~100 discrete findings captured as actionable tickets. No implementation — discovery and documentation only.

title: [D] Missing spec for ingestMany() partial-failure error shape
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md — requires ingestMany() with partial-failure semantics returning settled IngestManyResult[]. The spec does not define what TRAGarError.code should be for an individual item failure within ingestMany().
**Why it matters**: Implementors and callers cannot code to a contract that does not define the per-item error model.
**What to do**: Extend the public-api spec to define which ErrorCodes are valid in a per-item rejection and whether the rejection reason is always TRAGarError or can be any Error.

title: [D] browser-compat spec requires startup feature-detection; create() does no capability checks
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: browser-compat/spec.md §Required Browser Features — 'tRAGar MUST check for their availability at startup and reject with an appropriate error if any is missing' (WebAssembly+SIMD, BigInt, Promise.allSettled, OPFS or IDB, crypto.subtle.digest); §SIMD Hard Requirement — 'refuse to load with an Internal error … SIMD required'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:768-792 (create()) — opens store, validates namespace, returns instance; no capability checks for WebAssembly, BigInt, Promise.allSettled, or crypto.subtle
**Mismatch**: Spec requires a startup capability gate that rejects with typed errors if browser features are missing. Code has zero feature detection.
**Why it matters**: Library silently fails later (likely with cryptic errors) in unsupported browsers instead of giving clear rejection at create() time.
**What to do**: Add a capabilities check function called at start of create(); throw StoreUnavailable if neither OPFS nor IDB, throw Internal('SIMD required') if WASM SIMD absent.

title: [B] transformers embedder: embed() failure mid-batch not tested
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-transformers.test.ts (missing test); js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:55-64
**Issue**: The EmbedderLoadFailed tests simulate pipeline() factory throwing (load failure), but do not simulate the case where pipeline is successfully loaded but the pipeline function itself throws during embed() (i.e. loadPipeline succeeds, but pipe(batch, ...) rejects). This second failure path is unexercised.
**Why it matters**: Network or memory errors during inference (not during model load) should also surface as TRAGarError, but the current code would propagate the raw error untyped.
**What to do**: Add a test where pipeline() factory succeeds but the returned function throws on every call; verify the error propagated is TRAGarError('EmbedderLoadFailed') or a documented raw error.

title: [B] queryStream() throw path not tested for persistent (opfs/file) store
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-querystream.test.ts (missing tests); js/tests/slice-persistence.test.ts (missing tests)
**Issue**: All queryStream() error tests (InstanceClosed, InvalidConfig) only test the memory store path (TRAGarMemoryInstance). TRAGarPersistentInstance also implements queryStream() (tragar.ts:581-603) with the same guards but these are never exercised in the persistence test suite.
**Why it matters**: TRAGarPersistentInstance.queryStream() is duplicated code with its own guards. If the persistent implementation diverges (e.g. a future PR patches only one class), the other class's error behavior is undetected.
**What to do**: Add tests in slice-persistence.test.ts for queryStream() after close() and queryStream() without embedder on the opfs store.

title: [D] IdbFileBackend uses single object store tragar.{ns}.blobs — spec requires two stores
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: store-opfs/spec.md §IndexedDB Fallback — 'The fallback uses two object stores: tragar.{namespace}.blobs — keys are filenames, values are ArrayBuffers; tragar.{namespace}.chunks — keys are chunk IDs, values are chunk records'
**Code**: js/seams/opfs-store.ts:103-113 (openIndexedDb) — creates only one object store: tragar.{namespace}.blobs; no .chunks store created
**Mismatch**: Spec requires two IndexedDB object stores; code creates only one. Chunk records are stored via the blob store using JSON-serialized chunks.jsonl, not the dedicated .chunks store.
**Why it matters**: The two-store design in the spec allows direct chunk-by-ID lookups without full file deserialization; missing store breaks spec contract for implementations that depend on this.
**What to do**: Create the tragar.{namespace}.chunks object store in openIndexedDb; decide whether to use it or document the architectural divergence.

title: [B] TRAGarError.name property is never asserted in any test
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/errors.ts:9; all test files (missing assertion)
**Issue**: The TRAGarError constructor sets this.name = 'TRAGarError' (errors.ts:9), but no test anywhere verifies that error.name === 'TRAGarError'. Tests check instanceof and .code but not .name. The .name property is critical for serialization (JSON.stringify, logging) and for error filtering in frameworks that check error.name.
**Why it matters**: If TRAGarError is subclassed or the name assignment is removed in a refactor, no test catches the regression. Callers using err.name in logging or display code would see 'Error' instead.
**What to do**: Add assertion in the existing close()-after-close test in bootstrap.test.ts: expect((err as TRAGarError).name).toBe('TRAGarError').

title: [D] no spec exists for export() method added by add-vector-access-export change
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: add-vector-access-export/proposal.md mentions export(format) but no spec file covers export() semantics; public-api/spec.md has no export() section
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:328-364, 640-676 — export(format: 'json' | 'binary') is fully implemented in both instance classes; js/types.ts:213-219 — TRAGarInstance interface documents export()
**Mismatch**: Code implements export() with specific JSON structure (meta, chunks, vectors_b64) and binary ZIP format, but there is no corresponding spec to validate correctness of the format, the ZIP structure, or error behavior on empty corpus.
**Why it matters**: Without a spec, there is no contract for consumers of the export format; format can change silently across versions.
**What to do**: Write a spec section in public-api/spec.md (or a new spec file) defining the export() method: supported formats, JSON schema, ZIP entry names, behavior on empty corpus, and Blob MIME types.

title: [B] No test for namespace isolation — two namespaces should not share chunks
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice2.test.ts, js/tests/slice-persistence.test.ts (missing tests)
**Issue**: No test creates two TRAGar instances with different namespaces on the same backend and verifies their data is isolated. The memory store uses separate in-memory arrays per instance by construction, but a persistent backend uses separate file paths keyed by namespace. No test validates cross-namespace isolation.
**Why it matters**: A bug where namespace is ignored in the path construction (e.g. always writing to 'default') would let data bleed across namespaces silently. This is a critical correctness property.
**What to do**: Create two instances with namespaces 'ns-a' and 'ns-b' on the same MemoryFileBackend (opfs store), ingest different docs to each, then verify each instance's query only returns its own docs.

title: [D] write serialization queue not implemented — concurrent ingest() calls can corrupt store
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Instance Lifecycle — 'Write serialization: The instance queues concurrent write operations (ingest, ingestMany, compact, clear, delete) internally so they execute in call order. Concurrent calls do not corrupt store state.'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:228-243, 538-557 — ingest() has no mutex or serialization queue; two concurrent calls can interleave #appendChunks writes
**Mismatch**: Spec requires internal serialization of concurrent writes. Code does not serialize; concurrent ingest() calls can produce interleaved chunks.jsonl writes on the persistent store.
**Why it matters**: A user calling ingest() in a Promise.all() without knowing about the requirement could corrupt their persistent store.
**What to do**: Add a private write-queue (promise chain or mutex) to TRAGarPersistentInstance; serialize all write operations through it.
close_reason: Duplicate of tRAG-566 — same root cause (no mutex on #appendChunks), same fix

title: [B] MemoryFileBackend.exists() is never directly tested
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-persistence.test.ts (missing test); js/seams/opfs-store.ts:31-33
**Issue**: MemoryFileBackend has three methods: read(), write(), exists(). Only read() and write() are exercised indirectly through the persistence roundtrip tests. exists() is called inside TRAGarPersistentInstance but there is no test that directly verifies exists() returns false for a missing key and true for a written key.
**Why it matters**: If exists() regresses (e.g. always returns false), the persistence layer silently fails to detect pre-existing namespaces; the error only surfaces as wrong count after reopen.
**What to do**: Add unit tests for MemoryFileBackend directly: test that exists() returns false before write, true after write, and false again if the key is different.

title: [B] Browser tracer-bullet test has no assertions about query result content
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: tests/browser/tracer-bullet.spec.ts:32-36
**Issue**: The browser E2E test only checks that #output has class 'ok' and contains 'All checks passed'. It does not verify any specific query results, hit scores, or that ingest actually stored retrievable data. The 'All checks passed' message is produced by the tracer-bullet example's own self-check, which is a black box to the test.
**Why it matters**: If the tracer-bullet example's self-check is too lenient (e.g. passes even when hits array is empty), the E2E test cannot catch regressions in the actual RAG pipeline.
**What to do**: Either expose structured result data from the example page (e.g. a data attribute with hit count) and assert on it in the Playwright test, or add additional assertions like checking that a specific hit source appears in the output text.

title: [B] Native C++ test covers only StoreMode::Memory — no InvalidConfig test
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: tests/native/test_bootstrap.cpp; src/core/tragar_core.hpp:16-17
**Issue**: The C++ core defines ErrorCode::InvalidConfig but the test file has zero test cases exercising it. The only tested error code is AlreadyClosed (line 48-50). There is no test for invalid namespace validation, invalid config combinations, or any other InvalidConfig path at the C++ level.
**Why it matters**: The C++ core will eventually be used by the WASM path; untested error codes in the native layer mean the WASM binding may silently swallow these errors.
**What to do**: Add TEST_CASE for invalid namespace (empty string, too long) and any other InvalidConfig scenario documented in the spec, verifying result.error() == ErrorCode::InvalidConfig.

title: [D] embedders.custom() factory has wrong parameter order vs spec
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Seam Factory API — 'embedders.custom(fn, dim, modelId, opts?)' — fn is first argument
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:744-749 — embedders.custom(embed, dim, modelId) — same order, BUT spec also mentions an optional fourth argument opts?: { silent?: boolean } that code lacks entirely
**Mismatch**: The optional fourth argument opts with silent flag to suppress network-call warning is missing from the implementation.
**Why it matters**: Callers following the spec docs will pass a fourth argument that is silently ignored.
**What to do**: Add optional opts?: { silent?: boolean } fourth parameter to embedders.custom() factory; propagate to returned config.

title: [B] stores.file() with relative path is untested
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-file-store.test.ts (missing test); source at tragar.ts:725-733
**Issue**: All file store tests use absolute temp directory paths. The stores.file() factory accepts any string including relative paths (e.g. './data'). The NodeFsFileBackend joins paths with path.join(), which resolves relative paths from cwd. No test covers relative paths.
**Why it matters**: Relative paths resolve differently depending on cwd at test time vs. production; callers using relative paths could write to unexpected locations silently.
**What to do**: Add a test that calls stores.file('./tragar-relative-test') and either verifies it rejects with a clear error or resolves to the expected absolute path.

title: [B] queryStream() 'InvalidConfig' error code not verified — only instance checked
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-querystream.test.ts:271-278
**Issue**: The test 'throws TRAGarError(InvalidConfig) when no embedder is configured' only verifies toBeInstanceOf(TRAGarError) and the .code property. However, the InstanceClosed error test at line 258-269 also verifies .code. The InvalidConfig test (line 274-278) is a separate describe block and does check .code. On re-reading: both tests DO check code. But queryStream() 'InstanceClosed' test doesn't close the instance — it has the db open at ingest time, then closes, but collectStream is called. This is correct. What is missing: queryStream() throws synchronously vs asynchronously. The source uses async generator — errors on first next() call, not on queryStream() call itself. There is no test verifying the stream errors on next() rather than on the queryStream() call.
**Why it matters**: If queryStream() is refactored to throw eagerly (not lazily), callers using try/catch around the queryStream() call (not around the iterator) would break.
**What to do**: Add a test explicitly wrapping just the queryStream() call in try/catch and verifying it does NOT throw; only the iteration should throw.

title: [D] meta.json missing language, chunkerConfig, embedderConfig fields from spec schema
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: store-opfs/spec.md §meta.json Schema — 'meta.json SHALL conform to shape at schema version 1' including: language: 'es'|'en'|'auto', chunkerConfig: {}, embedderConfig: {}
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:379-387 (Meta interface), js/tragar.ts:501-512 (#writeMeta) — Meta has schemaVersion, modelId, dim, count, rawCount, createdAt, updatedAt only
**Mismatch**: Three required schema fields (language, chunkerConfig, embedderConfig) are absent from the Meta interface and never written to disk.
**Why it matters**: A future library version reading a namespace written now will not have language or config context for migration decisions.
**What to do**: Add language, chunkerConfig, embedderConfig to the Meta interface and populate them (with sensible defaults: 'auto', {}, {}) in #writeMeta.

title: [F] OpenSpec has no seam-extension capability spec — custom seam contracts undocumented
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: OpenSpec workflow / seam extensibility
**Issue**: openspec/specs/ has capability specs for browser-compat, chunker-markdown, embedder-transformers, error-model, index-flat, quantization, store-opfs, public-api, and tracer-bullet-demo. There is no capability spec for 'seam-extension-protocol' or 'custom-embedder'/'custom-store' contracts. The embedders.custom() and FileBackend injection points exist in code but have no formal requirement or scenario coverage in any spec file.
**Risk**: New contributors implementing custom seams have no spec to validate against. OpenSpec change proposals for new seam types (e.g., add-clusterer-seam, add-projector-seam which are active changes) cannot reference a base seam-extension spec, risking inconsistency across seam proposals.
**What to do**: Create openspec/specs/seam-extension/spec.md defining: the Embedder interface contract, the FileBackend interface contract, validation requirements (dim consistency, error wrapping), and at least one scenario per seam type. Link from public-api spec.

title: [B] No test for concurrent ingest() calls on the same instance
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice2.test.ts, js/tests/slice-persistence.test.ts (missing tests)
**Issue**: All tests call ingest() sequentially (await one, then the other). The source #appendChunks() in TRAGarPersistentInstance (tragar.ts:514-535) reads then writes chunks.jsonl in two separate async operations — concurrent ingests could race and corrupt the JSONL file. No test exercises concurrent ingest() calls.
**Why it matters**: Real applications may ingest multiple documents in parallel (Promise.all). A race in #appendChunks would silently lose chunks.
**What to do**: Add a test: await Promise.all([db.ingest(DOC_A), db.ingest(DOC_B), db.ingest(DOC_C)]) on a persistent store, verify count equals 3 after all resolve.

title: [D] onWarn omission defaults missing — spec requires console.warn fallback
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Warning Callback — 'When omitted, warnings MUST be forwarded to console.warn'
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:193-206, 407-424 — #onWarn stored as-is; when undefined the optional-chaining ?. silently swallows warnings
**Mismatch**: Spec says omitting onWarn must route warnings to console.warn. Code uses onWarn?.() which drops warnings silently when onWarn is not provided.
**Why it matters**: Developers get no warnings about StoreFallback, oversized chunks, etc. in the default path, which is exactly the case most needing visibility.
**What to do**: In both constructors, default #onWarn to a function that calls console.warn(code, message).

title: [A] TRAGarError stack trace truncated — missing Object.setPrototypeOf for custom Error subclasses
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/errors.ts:6-13
**Issue**: TRAGarError extends Error but does not call Object.setPrototypeOf(this, TRAGarError.prototype) in the constructor. In TypeScript projects compiled to ES5 targets (or with certain tsconfig settings), extending built-in types like Error requires this explicit prototype fixup. Without it, instanceof TRAGarError checks can fail, returning false for thrown TRAGarErrors. Even in modern targets the omission is a known source of subtle bugs.
**Why it matters**: Code doing 'catch (e) { if (e instanceof TRAGarError)' may silently fall through to the wrong branch in some build configurations, swallowing the typed error.
**What to do**: Add Object.setPrototypeOf(this, new.target.prototype) immediately after the super() call in the TRAGarError constructor.

title: [F] No stable vs experimental surface annotation in public types
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: API stability / public contract
**Issue**: SPEC §19.3 lists surfaces unstable in v0.1 (error code list, Stats shape, storage layout, default model). However, types.ts and tragar.ts carry no @public/@beta/@alpha TSDoc annotations, and there is no TSDoc @experimental or @deprecated tag on the getVector/getAllVectors methods (which fire a DequantizationRequested warning hinting they are provisional). Consumers cannot tell from the types alone which surface is stable.
**Risk**: Adopters treat the entire surface as stable or, conversely, avoid all of it. When the Stats shape or error codes change at v0.2, there is no machine-readable signal that they were unstable.
**What to do**: Add TSDoc stability tags to types.ts; annotate unstable surfaces listed in SPEC §19.3 with @beta or a custom @unstable tag; consider using TypeScript declaration emit to expose a stability matrix in the published package.

title: [B] slice-vector-access.test.ts uses module-level beforeEach without afterEach close()
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-vector-access.test.ts:30-36
**Issue**: The module-level 'db' variable is initialized in beforeEach() but there is no afterEach() that calls db.close(). Tests that don't close 'db' manually (most of them) leave the instance open. The 'closed instance' describe block at line 191 calls db.close() inside the test, which means subsequent tests in the same suite that reuse a stale 'db' reference could fail if beforeEach order is not reset.
**Why it matters**: Unreleased instances can mask resource-leak bugs and make test failures order-dependent.
**What to do**: Add afterEach(() => db.close().catch(() => {})) to the top-level scope so every test that doesn't close manually is cleaned up.

title: [F] WASM binary not built or checked in — no reproducible artifact
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: build reproducibility / release
**Issue**: CMakeLists.txt shows the C++23 core compiles to WASM via Emscripten (src/bind/), but no .wasm artifact is committed to the repo and the JS package.json has no build script that produces one. The current JS implementation in tragar.ts is pure JS (Slice 2 note: 'no WASM in this slice'). There is no documented CI step to produce or cache the WASM binary, no version pinning of the expected binary, and no integrity hash.
**Risk**: When the C++ path lands, there is no reproducibility guarantee: different Emscripten versions produce different binaries, and no policy exists for which version is canonical. Consumers who build from source cannot verify they match a released binary.
**What to do**: Document the Emscripten version required in CMakePresets.json or a toolchain file; add a justfile recipe for WASM build; define a policy for whether the .wasm is checked in or downloaded at install time (with integrity hash).

title: [F] @xenova/transformers is a runtime dependency — not tree-shakeable
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: bundle size / dependency strategy
**Issue**: @xenova/transformers is listed as a hard runtime dependency in js/package.json rather than a peer or optional dependency. The entire transformers.js module (~several MB including ONNX WASM binaries) is pulled in even when callers only use embedders.custom() or a future third-party embedder. The ONNX WASM files (.wasm, ~4 variants) in node_modules confirm the full weight is present.
**Risk**: Any bundler that does not support dynamic-import tree-shaking will include the full transformers bundle unconditionally. The SPEC goal of 'one .wasm + one .js' from a static host is undermined by a bundled embedder.
**What to do**: Move @xenova/transformers to peerDependencies or optionalDependencies; document in README that it is only required when using embedders.transformers(); verify the dynamic import in transformers-embedder.ts correctly lazy-loads when peer dep is absent.

title: [A] dotProduct() does not check that vectors have equal length
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:85-88
**Issue**: dotProduct(a, b) iterates from 0 to a.length without checking that b.length >= a.length. If a stored chunk has dim=384 but a query vector has dim=128 (due to a misconfigured embedder), the loop silently reads undefined for b[i] past index 127, producing NaN scores. Similarly l2Normalize operates on whatever vector it receives with no size validation.
**Why it matters**: Produces silently corrupt similarity scores rather than a detectable error. Combined with the missing dim validation noted in tRAG-3yt, this creates a multi-layer silent failure.
**What to do**: In dotProduct(), assert a.length === b.length (throw if not, or add a debug-mode assertion). This is complementary to the validation proposed in tRAG-3yt.

title: [B] DequantizationRequested warning never tested for getVector() first call
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-vector-access.test.ts:89-119
**Issue**: The warning tests only cover the case where getAllVectors() fires the warning first, then getVector() does NOT re-fire it. There is no test for the complementary path: getVector() being called FIRST (before getAllVectors()), which should fire the warning exactly once.
**Why it matters**: The two code paths (#fireDequantWarn() called from both getVector and getAllVectors) each hold the same guard. If the guard is mistakenly removed from getVector(), the existing test will not catch it.
**What to do**: Add a test: fresh instance, call getVector() only (not getAllVectors()), verify 'DequantizationRequested' is in warns exactly once.

title: [F] TRAGarMemoryInstance and TRAGarPersistentInstance share duplicated logic
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: maintainability / code structure
**Issue**: Both classes in tragar.ts duplicate ~200 lines of identical logic: #assertOpen, #requireEmbedder, ingest, query, queryStream, stats, #fireDequantWarn, getVector, getAllVectors, and export. They differ only in whether #appendChunks persists to a backend. The duplication means bug fixes and new methods must be applied twice.
**Risk**: Divergence between the two implementations over time (already present: close() throws differently in memory vs persistent). Adds maintenance burden for every new feature on TRAGarInstance.
**What to do**: Extract shared logic into a base class or a standalone module (e.g., a ChunkStore helper that both implementations delegate to). Ensure the two constructors remain distinct for the store-wiring difference.
close_reason: Duplicate of tRAG-cxg — same observation (Memory/Persistent duplication), same recommendation

title: [B] export('json') on empty corpus is untested — possible dim=0 bug
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-vector-access.test.ts (missing test); source at tragar.ts:341
**Issue**: export('json') uses 'const dim = this.dim || (this.#chunks[0]?.vector.length ?? 0)' — when dim is 0 (no embedder configured) AND corpus is empty, dim falls back to 0. The resulting vectorData is a zero-length Float32Array. No test covers export() on an empty corpus.
**Why it matters**: An empty corpus export should produce valid JSON with empty chunks and vectors_b64; if it throws or produces malformed output, downstream deserialization breaks silently.
**What to do**: Add tests: export('json') on a fresh empty instance and export('binary') on empty instance — both should resolve to valid Blobs with count=0.

title: [A] TRAGarError does not set Error.cause, losing original error context
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/errors.ts:6-13; js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:41-46; js/seams/fs-store.ts:41-43, 63-64
**Issue**: When catching errors and re-throwing as TRAGarError, the original error is stringified into the message but not passed as Error.cause. For example, in transformers-embedder.ts the caught err is only used for its .message, and in fs-store.ts only err.message is forwarded. The TRAGarError constructor does not accept a cause parameter and does not pass { cause } to super().
**Why it matters**: Error.cause is the standard mechanism for chaining errors (ES2022). Without it, stack traces from the root cause are lost, making debugging difficult. Logging tools and error reporters that inspect .cause get nothing.
**What to do**: Add an optional cause?: unknown parameter to TRAGarError's constructor and pass it as { cause } to super(). Update all catch-and-rethrow sites to pass the original error.
close_reason: Duplicate of tRAG-iuh — both cover TRAGarError missing Error.cause field, same fix

title: [B] IngestDoc.meta field is never tested — silently dropped
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice2.test.ts (missing test); types.ts:98-100; tragar.ts:228-242
**Issue**: The IngestDoc interface declares an optional 'meta' field (Record<string, unknown>), but the current implementation never stores or returns it. No test verifies this field's behavior — whether it is stored, ignored, or propagated to Hit objects.
**Why it matters**: The meta field is part of the public API contract. If an implementation later tries to store it, there is no test to verify the round-trip. If it is intentionally a no-op, there is no test documenting that decision.
**What to do**: Add a test passing meta: { tag: 'important' } to ingest() and verify the behavior is defined (either propagated in hits or explicitly ignored per spec).

title: [F] Index and Reranker seams absent from public API and types
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: seam extensibility / public API
**Issue**: SPEC §4.3 defines five seams: chunker, embedder, store, index, reranker. Only embedder and store have public TypeScript interfaces and factory methods. Index and Reranker seams are entirely absent from types.ts and the TRAGar export object. CreateConfig has no index or reranker properties.
**Risk**: The 'five composable seams' goal (SPEC §2.1 goal 3) is not achievable without forking. Users who need custom scoring or reranking have no stable surface to code against.
**What to do**: Add Index and Reranker interfaces to types.ts; add stubs (or at minimum placeholder types) to CreateConfig; document which seams are available vs deferred to v0.2+.

title: [E] NodeFsFileBackend.read() re-imports node modules on every call
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/fs-store.ts:26-33 and 37-44 and 47-53
**Scenario**: Every call to read(), write(), and exists() on NodeFsFileBackend calls await loadNodeModules() which does Promise.all([import('node:fs/promises'), import('node:path')]). Although Node.js module cache means import() resolves quickly after the first call, the async overhead is incurred on every read/write call — potentially hundreds of times during a large ingest.
**Risk**: Unnecessary async overhead per storage operation; more importantly the dynamic import inside a method makes the backend structurally dependent on module resolution succeeding at call time rather than at construction time, so a broken Node environment would fail on the first write rather than at backend construction.
**What to do**: Load fs and path once in openFileBackend() and pass them into NodeFsFileBackend's constructor, or cache the result of loadNodeModules() in a module-level variable.

title: [B] SchemaTooOld error path is untested
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice-persistence.test.ts (missing test); source at tragar.ts:475-480
**Issue**: The persistence code throws TRAGarError('SchemaTooOld') when meta.schemaVersion < SCHEMA_VERSION (currently 1). The 'SchemaTooNew' path is tested at line 287-308, but there is no equivalent test for schemaVersion = 0.
**Why it matters**: The SchemaTooOld guard is untested dead code. If the condition is accidentally inverted or the message is wrong, no test catches it.
**What to do**: Add a test that writes meta.json with schemaVersion: 0, opens TRAGar, and expects TRAGarError with code 'SchemaTooOld'.

title: [F] Chunker seam has no public TypeScript interface or factory
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: seam extensibility / public API
**Issue**: SPEC §4.3 lists Chunker as one of the five replaceable seams, but types.ts exports no Chunker interface and tragar.ts exports no TRAGar.chunkers factory. The only chunker in use is the hardcoded paragraphChunk() function. Users wanting a custom chunker (e.g., sentence-boundary, semantic) have no documented seam to implement against and no injection point in CreateConfig.
**Risk**: Forking required for any custom chunking strategy. API shape will be harder to stabilise before v1.0 because the seam has never been exercised publicly.
**What to do**: Define a Chunker interface in types.ts; add a chunkers namespace to the TRAGar export object; wire CreateConfig.chunker?: ChunkerConfig; write at least one scenario test against a custom chunker.

title: [A] IngestDoc.meta field is declared but never stored or exposed
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/types.ts:99, js/tragar.ts:241 and js/tragar.ts:551
**Issue**: IngestDoc declares an optional meta: Record<string, unknown> field described as 'caller-supplied metadata attached to every chunk from this doc.' Neither TRAGarMemoryInstance.ingest() nor TRAGarPersistentInstance.ingest() reads or stores this field. StoredChunk has no meta property. Hit has no meta property. The metadata is silently dropped on ingest.
**Why it matters**: This is a documented public API field that does nothing — callers who pass metadata will not notice it is ignored until they try to retrieve it. The Hit type should expose per-chunk metadata to be useful.
**What to do**: Either implement metadata propagation (StoredChunk, Hit, ChunkRecord all need a meta field) or remove the meta field from IngestDoc until the feature is implemented, with a note in the changelog.

title: [B] No test for query() when no embedder is configured (memory store)
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice2.test.ts (missing test); js/tests/slice-querystream.test.ts:271-278 has queryStream coverage but not query()
**Issue**: slice-querystream.test.ts tests queryStream() with no embedder and verifies TRAGarError(InvalidConfig), but the identical guard in query() (tragar.ts:246-247 #requireEmbedder) has no corresponding test in slice2.test.ts.
**Why it matters**: The guard exists in two code paths; if only one is tested, a refactor that removes one guard is not caught.
**What to do**: Add a test in slice2.test.ts: create instance without embedder, call query(), expect TRAGarError with code 'InvalidConfig'.

title: [F] @xenova/transformers v2 is deprecated; migrate to @huggingface/transformers
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: dependency / embedder seam
**Issue**: @xenova/transformers v2.17 is the deprecated predecessor package. The maintained successor is @huggingface/transformers (v3+). The package is pinned with ^2.17.0 in js/package.json, meaning it will never receive v3 features or security patches under the current range.
**Risk**: Accumulating lag against the upstream model-loading API; breaking changes in model hosting (HuggingFace Hub redirects) may affect v2 silently while v3 has fixes. Long-term, this becomes a forced migration under time pressure.
**What to do**: Plan migration to @huggingface/transformers v3; audit API surface differences (pipeline() call signature, quantized model support); target a v0.2 minor bump with the migration.

title: [B] No test for unicode / multi-byte content in ingest and query
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice2.test.ts, js/tests/slice-persistence.test.ts (missing tests)
**Issue**: All test documents use pure ASCII. chunkId() uses TextEncoder + SHA-256 (tragar.ts:55-61) and paragraphChunk() uses regex splitting. Neither path is tested with unicode text (e.g. CJK characters, emoji, RTL text).
**Why it matters**: TextEncoder serializes multi-byte characters; paragraph splitting on \n{2,} may behave differently with certain unicode line-ending variants (\u2028, \r\n). Chunk IDs could collide or be incorrect.
**What to do**: Add tests ingesting documents with CJK text, emoji, and mixed unicode + ASCII, then verify the chunk IDs are non-empty strings and queries return results.

title: [A] export('binary') uses schemaVersion: 1 magic number instead of SCHEMA_VERSION constant
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:332 (TRAGarMemoryInstance) and js/tragar.ts:643 (TRAGarPersistentInstance)
**Issue**: Both export() implementations hardcode schemaVersion: 1 in the export metadata object, instead of referencing the module-level SCHEMA_VERSION constant. When the schema version is bumped, export() will silently produce stale version tags while #loadFromStore correctly uses the constant.
**Why it matters**: Exported archives will carry an incorrect schema version, causing import failures when the schema version is incremented.
**What to do**: Replace the literal 1 with SCHEMA_VERSION in both export() implementations.

title: [F] No CHANGELOG.md — v0.x breaking changes undetectable
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: versioning / release process
**Issue**: SPEC §19.1 promises breaking changes are 'signaled in CHANGELOG' at v0.x minor bumps, but no CHANGELOG exists in the repo. Consumers pinning to ^0.1.0 have no changelog to diff.
**Risk**: Silent breaking changes land unannounced; adopters only notice at runtime. Erodes trust before v1.0 freeze.
**What to do**: Add CHANGELOG.md following Keep-a-Changelog format; add a 'changelog entry required' check to the PR template or CI.

title: [A] IdbFileBackend.read() only handles ArrayBuffer result, not Uint8Array
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/opfs-store.ts:83
**Issue**: IdbFileBackend.read() resolves to new Uint8Array(req.result) only when req.result instanceof ArrayBuffer. But IdbFileBackend.write() stores data.buffer.slice(0), which is a plain ArrayBuffer — correct. However, if the database was written by a different path or older code that stored a Uint8Array directly (which IndexedDB can store as a structured-clone), req.result would be a Uint8Array and the check would return null, silently acting as if the key doesn't exist.
**Why it matters**: A previously stored Uint8Array in IndexedDB would be invisible to read(), causing every open() to behave as a fresh namespace and silently discarding persisted data.
**What to do**: Broaden the check to: req.result instanceof Uint8Array ? req.result : req.result instanceof ArrayBuffer ? new Uint8Array(req.result) : null.

title: [B] query() with k=0 is untested — potential off-by-one or infinite loop
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice2.test.ts (missing test); source at tragar.ts:251,264
**Issue**: No test exercises query() with k=0. The source code does scored.slice(0, 0) which returns [] — but this is an edge case worth pinning. The spec says 'k defaults to 10' but does not explicitly document k=0 semantics.
**Why it matters**: k=0 is a valid caller mistake; without a test this path may regress to returning all results or throwing unexpectedly.
**What to do**: Add test: db.query('alpha', { k: 0 }) should resolve to an empty array without throwing.

title: [B] ingest() error code never verified — only instance checked
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice2.test.ts:125-128
**Issue**: The test 'rejects with TRAGarError after close()' only checks toBeInstanceOf(TRAGarError) but does NOT assert the error code is 'InstanceClosed'. If the error code changes or is wrong, this test still passes.
**Why it matters**: Consumers depend on error codes to take different actions; a wrong code silently breaks their error-handling switch.
**What to do**: Add .code check to every ingest-after-close test: expect((err as TRAGarError).code).toBe('InstanceClosed').

title: [B] No test for ingest() with empty text document
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tests/slice2.test.ts (missing test)
**Issue**: ingest() is never called with a document where text is empty string or whitespace-only. The source code at tragar.ts:232-233 calls paragraphChunk() and returns early if paragraphs.length === 0, but no test exercises this path.
**Why it matters**: Silent no-op on empty ingest could mask user errors; count should stay 0 and no error thrown, but this contract is untested.
**What to do**: Add a test: ingest a doc with text '' and assert count stays 0 and the call resolves without error. Also test whitespace-only text (' \n  ').

title: [A] loadNodeModules() called on every read/write/exists in NodeFsFileBackend
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/fs-store.ts:15-21, lines 27-28, 37-38, 47-48
**Issue**: loadNodeModules() does a dynamic Promise.all([import('node:fs/promises'), import('node:path')]) on every call to read(), write(), and exists(). Node.js module cache means the imports resolve quickly after the first call, but the overhead of two async dynamic import expressions per operation is unnecessary and the pattern is surprising to readers expecting a cached loader.
**Why it matters**: Performance overhead on hot paths (every chunk append calls write() and then another write() for meta); also, if the Node module cache were ever bypassed in certain bundler setups, this could cause real latency.
**What to do**: Cache the resolved { fs, path } object at module level (loaded once lazily) rather than re-awaiting on every method call.

title: [A] queryStream() return type is AsyncGenerator, interface requires AsyncIterable
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:267 and js/tragar.ts:581; js/types.ts:185
**Issue**: TRAGarInstance.queryStream() is typed as returning AsyncIterable<Hit> in the interface, but both implementations declare the return type as AsyncGenerator<Hit>. AsyncGenerator is a subtype of AsyncIterable, so TypeScript does not error, but this exposes internal generator semantics (return() / throw() methods) to callers via the concrete class, and any future refactoring that breaks the generator protocol would not be caught at the interface boundary.
**Why it matters**: It is an accidental interface leak. The declared interface contract (AsyncIterable) should be the only guarantee at call sites; callers should never see AsyncGenerator.
**What to do**: Annotate the implementations as returning AsyncIterable<Hit> to match the interface, or wrap in a plain async iterable adapter.

title: Plan tracer-bullet vertical slice for tRAGar
description:
Why: The repository has strong v0.1 and seam-expansion specs but no executable baseline slice proving the build, WASM boundary, JS API, and ingest/query loop work together.

Scope:

Non-goals:
- Do not absorb the 8 existing seam-expansion proposals

Outcome: a concrete, bounded proposal and plan that de-risks the first implementation work without widening scope.
design: Chosen direction: minimal/dev-first tracer bullet. Canonical bootstrap path is TRAGar.create({ store: TRAGar.stores.memory(), embedder: TRAGar.embedders.custom(...) }) plus ingest() and query() in a shipped browser example. queryStream, real persistence, and transformers.js are follow-on slices after the C++/Embind/TS seam is proven.

- WAI research, design, and plan artifacts document scope, architecture boundaries, and implementation sequencing for the tracer bullet
- Issue description, notes, and design explicitly define tracer-bullet scope, non-goals, deliverables, and relationship to the 8 existing seam-expansion OpenSpec changes
