title: Decide: default embedder model — English-only vs multilingual
description:
## Why this decision exists

tRAGar's target use case includes Spanish-language corpora (the dogfood demo, the companion essay). The v0.1 default is `Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (English-trained, ~23 MB, fast). The multilingual alternative is `Xenova/paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2` (~120 MB, better Spanish recall, slower first load).

The SPEC.md golden retrieval test sets the pass threshold at ≥80% top-1 accuracy on Spanish corpora — explicitly noting the English model is expected to degrade on Spanish and that the threshold will rise to ≥90% in v0.2 if the multilingual model becomes default.

## Current default

`Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` for v0.1. Multilingual available as a one-line opt-in.

## What needs to be decided

Whether the 5× model size penalty is worth it for the primary dogfood use case. Options:
1. Keep English default, multilingual opt-in (current plan).
2. Flip: multilingual default, English opt-in for size-constrained deploys.
3. Make the model configurable with no default (forces explicit choice at call site).

Decision affects the three-line happy path demo and the golden test threshold.
acceptance_criteria: The embedder-transformers spec records the chosen default model ID and the rationale. The embedder factory default matches.
notes: Decision (2026-04-30): Keep English default — Option 1. Rationale: 5× size penalty (120 MB vs 23 MB) not worth it for v0.1. Multilingual available as one-line opt-in. Threshold stays at ≥80% top-1; rises to ≥90% in v0.2 if we flip.
close_reason: Decided: keep Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 as English default, multilingual as opt-in

title: Decide: tokenizer for markdown chunker — approximation vs embedder's tokenizer
description:
## Why this decision exists

The markdown chunker needs to count tokens to stay within the embedder's context window (typically 256 or 512 tokens for `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`). Two approaches are viable:

- **Own approximation**: character-count heuristic (e.g., chars/4). Fast — no dependency on the embedder being loaded. Slightly inaccurate, may produce over-long chunks that the embedder silently truncates.
- **Embedder's tokenizer**: reuse the tokenizer bundled with transformers.js. Accurate — chunks are guaranteed to fit. Requires the embedder to be loaded before the chunker can run; adds ~5–10 ms per document depending on tokenizer overhead.

## Current default

Own approximation. Chosen for startup speed and chunker/embedder independence.

## What needs to be decided

Whether to keep the approximation for v0.1 or pay the cost of an accurate tokenizer. The spec says to reconsider after measurements. Decision gates the chunker implementation.
acceptance_criteria: A decision is recorded in the chunker-markdown spec with the rationale and any benchmark numbers that informed it. The chunker implementation matches the decision.
close_reason: Confirmed chars/4 approximation for v0.1. Fast, no dependency on embedder being loaded, acceptable inaccuracy for target chunk sizes. Accurate tokenizer (embedder's own) reconsidered after measurements in v0.2.

title: [F] No observability — no timing, structured logging, or diagnostics for ingest/query paths
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Area**: js/tragar.ts, public API
**Issue**: There is no timing instrumentation (elapsed ms per ingest, embed latency, store write latency), no structured debug logging, and no diagnostic API for understanding performance behavior. The only visibility into what is happening internally is a single onWarn callback.
**Risk**: Users cannot diagnose slow ingest pipelines or understand where latency comes from. Performance regressions in embedder or store layers are invisible.
**What to do**: Consider adding optional debug/timing metadata to IngestResult (once it exists), or an onDebug callback similar to onWarn; at minimum emit timing in IngestResult per spec.

title: [D] quantization spec mandates int8 storage — code stores float32 vectors in JSON arrays
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: quantization/spec.md — full int8 quantization procedure required before storage; per-vector scale stored; cosine recovery via int8 dot product; 4x storage reduction required
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:174-179 (StoredChunk.vector: Float32Array), js/tragar.ts:239-241 — stores raw Float32Array after only l2Normalize; ChunkRecord.vector: number[] serialized as JSON
**Mismatch**: Spec requires symmetric per-vector int8 quantization with separate scale storage. Code stores raw float32 vectors as JSON number arrays — no quantization, no int8, 4× larger than spec target.
**Why it matters**: Storage bloat (4× vs spec), SIMD int8 dot product path never exercised, NaN/Inf rejection missing, DegenerateVector warning never fires.
**What to do**: Note JS-only slice limitation in spec or create a separate JS-layer spec that defers quantization to the C++ path and documents the float32 interim storage format.

title: [D] chunker is paragraph-split only — spec requires full CommonMark/MD4C markdown chunker
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: chunker-markdown/spec.md — full spec for CommonMark parsing, heading boundary splits, token size limits, code block atomicity, list handling, Spanish language awareness, line number metadata
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:66-71 (paragraphChunk) — splits on /\n{2,}/, trims, drops empties; no markdown parsing, no heading detection, no token limits, no code block atomicity, no line numbers, no meta passthrough
**Mismatch**: Spec defines a sophisticated markdown chunker; code has a 6-line paragraph splitter that treats all text identically.
**Why it matters**: Core retrieval quality depends on proper chunk boundaries; paragraph splitting will merge code blocks, ignore heading structure, and produce differently-sized chunks than the spec requires.
**What to do**: Either implement chunker-markdown spec or expose the chunker as an injectable seam (chunkers.markdown(), chunkers.custom()) so spec compliance can be tracked separately from the core.

title: [C] Hit.score documented as 'cosine similarity in [0, 1]' but SPEC says [-1, 1]
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/types.ts:116 vs SPEC.md:557
**Issue**: The JSDoc comment on `Hit.score` in types.ts says 'Cosine similarity score in [0, 1]. Higher is more relevant.' SPEC §11.3 defines score as type `number` with comment 'cosine similarity, [-1, 1]'. For unit-normalized vectors, cosine similarity is mathematically bounded to [-1, 1], not [0, 1]. The current implementation uses dot product of L2-normalized vectors which can be negative.
**Why it matters**: Users who filter or display scores assuming [0, 1] will produce incorrect results for semantically dissimilar chunks whose score is negative. The documentation creates a false lower bound.
**What to do**: Update the JSDoc on Hit.score to say '[-1, 1]' and match the SPEC. If the intent is to clamp or shift to [0, 1], that needs to be implemented and documented explicitly.

title: [C] SPEC §2.2 non-goals state 'Node not tested' but stores.file() is a Node.js feature
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: SPEC.md:52 (§2.2 Non-goals point 6)
**Issue**: SPEC §2.2.6 states 'Not Node-first. v0.1 is browser-first. Node compatibility is best-effort and not tested.' However, `stores.file()` (implemented in js/seams/fs-store.ts, exposed in tragar.ts, and specified in openspec/changes/add-file-store/) is explicitly a Node.js-only feature with CI requirements (the file-store spec requires a Node 22 LTS CI job). The non-goal directly contradicts a deployed, spec-approved feature.
**Why it matters**: A reader evaluating whether tRAGar supports their Node.js build pipeline will see this non-goal and conclude it does not — even though stores.file() exists for exactly that purpose.
**What to do**: Update SPEC §2.2.6 to reflect the actual status: 'Not Node-first for query. Browser is the primary runtime. Node.js is supported as a build-time corpus-generation environment via stores.file().' Remove or qualify the 'not tested' claim.

title: [D] getVector/getAllVectors not gated on InstanceClosed per spec — missing assertOpen check
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: public-api/spec.md §Instance Lifecycle — 'After close(), all further method calls MUST reject with StoreUnavailable'; add-vector-access-export proposal — getVector rejects with TRAGarError('NotFound') if id is unknown
**Code**: js/tragar.ts:314-319, 322-325 (memory); js/tragar.ts:626-632, 634-638 (persistent) — both getVector and getAllVectors call #assertOpen() correctly
**Mismatch**: Actually the assertOpen check IS present. However, getVector() fires the DequantizationRequested warning even when the instance is empty (zero chunks). The warning message hardcodes float32 as 'native store format in this slice' — a stale reference to a development slice, not appropriate for a public API surface.
**Why it matters**: Stale internal 'slice' language leaks through the public warning message; confuses library users.
**What to do**: Update DequantizationRequested warning message to remove 'in this slice' language; use production-appropriate phrasing.

title: [C] SPEC §4.2 dataflow references int8 quantization but current JS impl stores float32
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: SPEC.md:89-107 (§4.2 Dataflow), js/tragar.ts:238-241
**Issue**: SPEC §4.2 describes the ingestion dataflow ending with 'C++ unit-normalizes each vector, then quantizes to int8 with a per-vector float32 scale.' The current JS-only implementation (tragar.ts lines 238-241) stores raw `Float32Array` — no quantization is performed. The SPEC dataflow description reads as if it applies to the current system, but it only describes the future WASM path.
**Why it matters**: A reader of §4.2 will believe the system quantizes to int8; a developer investigating memory usage will be confused when they find float32 storage. The gap is especially acute for the getVector/getAllVectors spec, which says 'dequantize stored int8 vectors on the fly' — the code just copies a float32 array.
**What to do**: Add a visible note at the start of §4.2 (or in a new §4.3 'Implementation status') stating that the JS-only v0.1 tracer bullet stores float32 vectors; int8 quantization is part of the C++23/WASM path in v0.2.

title: [C] IngestDoc.meta field defined in types.ts but never stored or surfaced on Hit
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/types.ts:93-100 (IngestDoc), js/types.ts:108-118 (Hit), js/tragar.ts:228-243 (ingest)
**Issue**: `IngestDoc` declares an optional `meta?: Record<string, unknown>` field. The Hit interface does not include a meta field. The ingest() implementation never reads `doc.meta` and never stores it on the chunk. The field is accepted silently and discarded. SPEC §5.4 defines `Chunk.meta` as 'user-provided pass-through' and SPEC §11.3 defines `Hit.meta: Record<string, unknown>`.
**Why it matters**: Users who pass metadata in IngestDoc expecting it to appear on query results will get undefined and no error. This is a silent data loss bug with no documentation warning.
**What to do**: Either (a) implement metadata pass-through from IngestDoc through StoredChunk to Hit, or (b) remove the meta field from IngestDoc and add a JSDoc note that metadata pass-through is not yet implemented.

title: [D] transformers embedder uses normalize:true — spec says vectors must NOT be pre-normalized
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**Spec**: embedder-transformers/spec.md §Batch Processing — Scenario: Vectors not pre-normalized: 'the returned vector may have L2 norm != 1.0 (normalization is deferred to the core)'
**Code**: js/seams/transformers-embedder.ts:57 — pipe(batch, { pooling: 'mean', normalize: true }) — sets normalize:true
**Mismatch**: Spec explicitly requires un-normalized output from embed(). Code passes normalize:true to the transformers.js pipeline, returning already-normalized vectors.
**Why it matters**: tRAGar then calls l2Normalize() again on the output; normalizing a unit vector is harmless but wasteful, and breaks the seam contract expected by the spec and any future embedder that relies on this guarantee.
**What to do**: Change normalize:true to normalize:false in the pipeline call; rely on tragar.ts l2Normalize() as specified.

title: [A] C++ ErrorCode enum missing parity with JS ErrorCode type
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: src/core/tragar_core.hpp:13-16
**Issue**: The C++ ErrorCode enum only declares AlreadyClosed and InvalidConfig. The JS/TS ErrorCode type (types.ts:12-20) has eight codes: InstanceClosed, InvalidConfig, EmbedderLoadFailed, SchemaTooNew, SchemaTooOld, NamespaceLocked, NotFound, StoreUnavailable. When the C++ path lands (Slice 2 / future), this enum will need to cover the same set of error conditions. There is currently no mechanism ensuring the two stay in sync.
**Why it matters**: Future WASM/C++ integration will expose errors with only 2 codes while the JS layer expects 8, causing silent unmapped errors when the bridge is built.
**What to do**: Expand the C++ ErrorCode enum to match all JS ErrorCode values, or create a shared source-of-truth (e.g. a code-gen step from a single definition file) to keep them in sync.

title: [C] SPEC §6.5 defines three embedder error codes not in types.ts ErrorCode union
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: SPEC.md:279-283 vs js/types.ts:12-20
**Issue**: SPEC §6.5 states the embedder must reject with one of: `EmbedderUnavailable`, `EmbedderInputTooLong`, or `EmbedderRuntimeError`. Only `EmbedderLoadFailed` appears in types.ts. The three spec-defined codes are absent from the ErrorCode union. The code in tragar.ts line 755 references `EmbedderLoadFailed` in comments but no runtime code produces the spec-defined codes.
**Why it matters**: Applications that handle embedder failures by catching specific codes will silently miss errors. This is also a spec vs. code contract break on the embedder error path.
**What to do**: Add `EmbedderUnavailable`, `EmbedderInputTooLong`, and `EmbedderRuntimeError` to the ErrorCode union in types.ts (or update the spec to use EmbedderLoadFailed). Document when each is thrown in the inline comment on the type.

title: [C] store-opfs spec Purpose field is 'TBD' — never updated after archive
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: openspec/specs/store-opfs/spec.md:3, openspec/specs/error-model/spec.md:3
**Issue**: Both `openspec/specs/store-opfs/spec.md` and `openspec/specs/error-model/spec.md` have `## Purpose` set to 'TBD - created by archiving change add-initial-capabilities. Update Purpose after archive.' These were never updated. A 'TBD' Purpose in a deployed spec makes the capability hard to search and violates the OpenSpec convention that specs/[capability]/spec.md must have a meaningful Purpose.
**Why it matters**: Specs with TBD Purpose degrade discoverability. `openspec list --specs` and any spec summary will show uninformative placeholder text for two core capabilities.
**What to do**: Write a one-to-two sentence Purpose for each affected spec describing what the capability covers. store-opfs: browser persistence via OPFS with IndexedDB fallback. error-model: TRAGarError class and ErrorCode enumeration.

title: [C] export() binary format in code does not match spec — missing vectors.bin, scales.bin, lookup.bin, tombstones.bin
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:328-363 (TRAGarMemoryInstance.export), openspec/changes/add-vector-access-export/specs/public-api/spec.md Requirement: Corpus Export
**Issue**: The spec states the binary export MUST produce a zip containing `meta.json, vectors.bin, scales.bin, chunks.jsonl, lookup.bin, tombstones.bin` — the canonical store format. The actual implementation (tragar.ts lines 354-364) builds a zip with only two files: `meta.json` and `chunks.jsonl`. vectors.bin, scales.bin, lookup.bin, and tombstones.bin are all absent. Additionally, chunks.jsonl in the export uses `vector: number[]` (float32 JSON) rather than int8 binary as the canonical format requires.
**Why it matters**: Exported 'binary' blobs cannot be imported by a browser TRAGar instance using stores.opfs() as the spec promises — the roundtrip is broken.
**What to do**: Document the current export format as a known deviation from the spec and file an implementation issue, or update the spec to match the current simplified format and remove the byte-identical guarantee.

title: [C] SPEC §11.1 close() behavior documented as 'StoreUnavailable' but code throws 'InstanceClosed'
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: SPEC.md (public-api spec Requirement: Instance Lifecycle) vs js/tragar.ts:213-215, 366-371
**Issue**: The public-api spec states 'After close(), all further method calls MUST reject with StoreUnavailable.' The code throws `TRAGarError('InstanceClosed',...)` after close. InstanceClosed is not in the spec's ErrorCode table; StoreUnavailable is in the spec table but the code does not use it for this case. These describe the same situation with different error codes.
**Why it matters**: Error-handling code written against the spec (`catch StoreUnavailable`) will silently miss post-close errors in the current implementation (which throws InstanceClosed).
**What to do**: Decide which code is correct and update the other. If InstanceClosed is preferred, add it to the spec's ErrorCode table with its semantics and update the lifecycle requirement to reference it.

title: [A] stores.file() Node.js guard runs at config-creation time, not at create() time
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:726-731
**Issue**: stores.file() throws TRAGarError('InvalidConfig', ...) synchronously when called outside Node.js, at config-object construction time rather than when create() is called. This means the error is thrown before any async work begins (which is intentional by the comment), but it breaks the pattern of uniform error handling: all other TRAGarError paths in the library surface asynchronously from create(), ingest(), or query(). A caller that builds a config in one place and calls create() elsewhere will see the throw at an unexpected stack frame.
**Why it matters**: Inconsistent error delivery model — synchronous throw vs. async rejection — complicates error handling for callers and is undocumented.
**What to do**: Either keep the synchronous guard but document it prominently in the JSDoc as 'throws synchronously', or move the guard into the 'file' case of create() so all config errors are async.

title: [C] SPEC §11 Stats type differs from types.ts Stats interface
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: SPEC.md:563-572 vs js/types.ts:121-127
**Issue**: SPEC §11.3 defines Stats as `{ count, rawCount, storeBytes, modelId, dim, language, schemaVersion }`. types.ts defines Stats as `{ count, dim, modelId, storeMode, namespace }`. The spec version includes rawCount, storeBytes, language, and schemaVersion; the code version includes storeMode and namespace. These fields are mutually exclusive — 6 of the 9 total fields differ or are missing on one side.
**Why it matters**: Users reading the spec to understand what stats() returns will write code against fields that don't exist on the real object. This is a spec-vs-code contract break on a frequently-used diagnostic method.
**What to do**: Reconcile Stats between SPEC §11.3 and types.ts. Update whichever is behind the current decision. Add JSDoc to each field explaining its meaning.

title: [C] SPEC §11 ingest() signature diverges from types.ts and current implementation
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: SPEC.md:508, js/types.ts:169, js/tragar.ts:228
**Issue**: SPEC §11.2 defines `ingest(text: string, meta?: DocumentMeta): Promise<IngestResult>` with two arguments and an IngestResult return type. The actual implementation in tragar.ts and the TRAGarInstance interface in types.ts define `ingest(doc: IngestDoc): Promise<void>` — one IngestDoc object argument and void return. These are structurally incompatible. Additionally, SPEC §11.2 includes ingestMany() which does not exist in either types.ts or tragar.ts.
**Why it matters**: The spec and code describe different APIs. Any documentation or example written from the spec will fail against the real code and vice versa.
**What to do**: Decide the authoritative signature and update the other to match. If the code is current, update SPEC §11.2. If the spec is the target, open a separate implementation ticket and add a note to the spec marking ingest() as 'spec-ahead'.

title: [A] opfs-store: _backend with _simulateOpfsFailure=true silently ignores _backend
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/seams/opfs-store.ts:124-125
**Issue**: openPersistentBackend checks 'if (config._backend && !config._simulateOpfsFailure)'. If both _backend and _simulateOpfsFailure are set, the _backend is silently ignored and the code falls through to the failure simulation branch. This is a surprising interaction: a caller who sets both thinking '_backend will be used but I also want to test the fallback path' gets neither behavior clearly.
**Why it matters**: Test configurations that set both flags produce unexpected behavior with no warning, making the test seams hard to reason about.
**What to do**: If both are set, either throw an error (invalid test config) or document the precedence explicitly in the JSDoc with a note that _simulateOpfsFailure takes priority over _backend.

title: [C] TRAGar.create() has no JSDoc on the exported object or create() function
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:768-801
**Issue**: The public `TRAGar` export and the `create()` function have no JSDoc comments. The `create()` function is the single most important entry point in the library, yet it has no description, no @param for `config`, no @returns documenting what is returned, and no @throws documenting that it rejects with `TRAGarError('InvalidConfig')` on bad namespace or `TRAGarError('SchemaTooNew'/'SchemaTooOld')` on schema mismatch.
**Why it matters**: `TRAGar.create()` is the first function every user calls. Its absence from generated docs is the worst possible first impression of the API reference.
**What to do**: Add full JSDoc to `create()`: description, @param config, @returns, @throws for each error code it can produce, and a minimal @example.

title: [A] buildStoreZip local file header leaves version/flags/compression fields as zero
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts:118-169
**Issue**: buildStoreZip() builds a ZIP archive manually. The local file header (offset 4) leaves the version-needed field at 0x0000 (bytes 4-5) and the general-purpose bit flag at 0x0000 (bytes 6-7), and the compression method at 0x0000 (stored). These are technically valid for STORE-method ZIP, but the date/time fields (bytes 10-13 in local header, 12-15 in central directory) are also left as 0x0000, which some unzip tools interpret as a corrupt timestamp or reject entirely.
**Why it matters**: ZIP files exported by the library may be rejected or display errors in strict unzip implementations and archiver tools.
**What to do**: Set the version-needed field to 20 (2.0 — required for STORE) in both local and central headers, and either set a valid DOS timestamp or document that the zero-timestamp is intentional (reproducible builds).

title: [C] ErrorCode type missing 6 codes present in spec — types.ts vs SPEC §12 mismatch
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/types.ts:12-20
**Issue**: The `ErrorCode` type in types.ts defines 8 codes: InstanceClosed, InvalidConfig, EmbedderLoadFailed, SchemaTooNew, SchemaTooOld, NamespaceLocked, NotFound, StoreUnavailable. SPEC §12 and the error-model spec define 12 codes: StoreUnavailable, StoreCorrupt, SchemaTooNew, SchemaTooOld, ModelMismatch, EmbedderUnavailable, EmbedderInputTooLong, EmbedderRuntimeError, QuotaExceeded, NamespaceLocked, InvalidConfig, Internal. The codes InstanceClosed, EmbedderLoadFailed, and NotFound exist in code but not in spec; StoreCorrupt, ModelMismatch, EmbedderUnavailable, EmbedderInputTooLong, EmbedderRuntimeError, QuotaExceeded, and Internal exist in spec but not in code.
**Why it matters**: The type contract does not match the spec. Users writing error-handling code against types.ts will miss codes that the full system will throw.
**What to do**: Reconcile ErrorCode between types.ts and SPEC §12. Either update the spec to reflect the current implementation codes, or update types.ts to add the missing spec codes. Document each code's meaning inline.

title: [A] TRAGarMemoryInstance and TRAGarPersistentInstance are near-identical — duplicated logic
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/tragar.ts — both classes span lines 184-372 and 396-684
**Issue**: The two instance classes share almost identical implementations of: #assertOpen(), #requireEmbedder(), query(), queryStream(), stats(), #fireDequantWarn(), getVector(), getAllVectors(), export(), and close(). The only real differences are: the constructor parameters, how ingest() persists chunks, and the #loadFromStore()/#appendChunks() persistence logic. This results in ~200 lines of duplicated code across the file.
**Why it matters**: Any bug fix (e.g. tRAG-24h, tRAG-3yt, tRAG-v5n above) must be applied twice. The two copies have already diverged in minor ways (e.g. export() uses slightly different formatting). The maintenance burden grows with each slice.
**What to do**: Extract shared logic into a base class or helper module. TRAGarMemoryInstance and TRAGarPersistentInstance would then only override/implement the storage-specific parts.

title: [C] TRAGarError class has no JSDoc — code property undocumented in errors.ts
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/errors.ts:6-14
**Issue**: `TRAGarError` is the only error class users will encounter, but it has zero JSDoc. The `code` property has no documentation, no explanation of what ErrorCode values mean, and no @example showing how to handle it. The spec (§12) defines 12 error codes with distinct semantics; none of those semantics are surfaced in errors.ts or on the class itself.
**Why it matters**: Error-handling code requires knowing what codes to expect. Without docs on the class, users must read SPEC.md or types.ts to learn the error contract.
**What to do**: Add JSDoc to the TRAGarError class with: (1) a description, (2) @param docs for constructor arguments, (3) a @example showing `catch (e) { if (e instanceof TRAGarError && e.code === 'InstanceClosed') ... }`, and (4) a @see reference to the ErrorCode type.

title: [C] TRAGarInstance interface missing JSDoc @param and @returns on all methods
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: js/types.ts:154-219
**Issue**: The `TRAGarInstance` interface has doc comments on methods but none use formal @param or @returns tags. For example, `ingest()`, `query()`, `getVector()`, `getAllVectors()`, and `export()` lack @param documentation for their arguments. The `export()` method has no @throws tag despite the implementation throwing TRAGarError('InstanceClosed'). Generated API docs (via `just docs`) will be incomplete for every public method.
**Why it matters**: TypeScript consumers and generated API reference will have no parameter-level documentation for the core methods.
**What to do**: Add @param, @returns, and @throws JSDoc tags to every method on TRAGarInstance. Minimum: ingest, query, queryStream, getVector, getAllVectors, export, close.

title: [C] README claims WASM coming in v0.2 but implies JS-only now — no clarification
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: README.md:8 and README.md:26
**Issue**: The tagline says '(C++23 → WebAssembly coming in v0.2)' and the Features section says 'one .js file (WASM coming in v0.2)'. The SPEC and openspec/project.md however define the full C++23 + WASM architecture as the target. There is no README note explaining that the current distribution is a pure-JS tracer bullet, not the intended WASM build. A reader expecting to import a .wasm file will be confused.
**Why it matters**: Users evaluating tRAGar based on the WASM performance promises may be misled about the current state vs. the roadmap.
**What to do**: Add a 'Current State' callout box or badge that states 'v0.1 ships as pure TypeScript (no WASM); WASM C++23 core lands in v0.2.' and links to the relevant SPEC section.

title: [C] README Quick Start missing stores.file() and embedders.custom() examples
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: README.md (Quick Start section)
**Issue**: The README only shows `stores.memory()` and `embedders.transformers()`. Two shipped store/embedder factories — `stores.file()` (Node.js build-time use, added in recent commit) and `embedders.custom()` (testing/offline) — have no usage example anywhere in README. New users have no starting point for Node.js workflows.
**Why it matters**: `stores.file()` is a key differentiator for build-time corpus generation workflows. Without an example, it is effectively invisible.
**What to do**: Add a second Quick Start code block showing the Node.js pattern: `stores.file(dirPath)` + `embedders.custom()`, or link to a docs page that covers it.

title: [C] README Quick Start uses wrong ingest() signature
description:
**Epic**: tRAG-2hf
**File**: README.md:19
**Issue**: The Quick Start example calls `rag.ingest({ source: 'notes', text: yourMarkdown })` with a single IngestDoc object. However, SPEC §11.2 and the public-api spec define the method signature as `ingest(text: string, meta?: DocumentMeta)` — two separate arguments, not a single object. The README example will fail with the production API.
**Why it matters**: The Quick Start is the first thing a new user runs. A broken snippet destroys trust immediately.
**What to do**: Update the ingest call to `await rag.ingest(yourMarkdown, { source: 'notes' })` to match the spec signature.

title: Decide: ingest() scope — markdown-only vs binary file support
description:
## Why this decision exists

The `ingest()` API accepts a `source` (string path/URL) and content. The spec currently scopes v0.1 to markdown-only. The question is whether `ingest()` should also accept `ArrayBuffer | Blob` and handle extraction for PDF/DOCX, or remain strictly markdown-typed.

## Current default

Markdown-only for v0.1. PDF/DOCX extraction deferred as 'a separate library concern.'

## What needs to be decided

Confirm markdown-only is the right boundary for v0.1, or identify whether there's a minimal binary path (e.g., plain-text extraction via FileReader) worth including now. The concern is that relaxing the type signature later may be a breaking change if the chunker seam contract is too narrow.

This is mostly a scope decision — the answer is likely 'keep markdown-only' but it must be recorded so the public-api spec's type signatures are final before implementation.
acceptance_criteria: The public-api spec's ingest() signature is finalized with an explicit statement of what input types are accepted in v0.1 and what the upgrade path is for v0.2+.
close_reason: Confirmed markdown-only for v0.1. ingest() accepts { source: string; text: string; meta?: Record<string, unknown> }. Binary (PDF/DOCX) support deferred to v0.2. Recorded in public-api spec acceptance criteria.

title: Decide: reranker streaming contract — rerank-all-then-yield vs interleave
description:
## Why this decision exists

The spec includes a `queryStream()` method (or implies one via AsyncIterable<Hit>). When a reranker is configured, the top-K results from the index must be reranked before yielding to the caller — but 'before' is ambiguous:

- **Rerank-all-then-yield**: collect all top-K hits, pass the batch to the reranker, yield the reranked list. Latency: full rerank before first hit.
- **Interleave**: yield high-confidence hits immediately, rerank concurrently, adjust order as scores arrive. Lower time-to-first-hit, more complex implementation, potentially confusing order mutations.

## Current default

Rerank-all-then-yield. Noted in SPEC.md as the current default that 'may change after v0.3 ships a reranker.'

## What needs to be decided

Since v0.1 has no reranker (default is `none`), this decision is purely about ensuring the AsyncIterable<Hit> contract doesn't paint v0.3 into a corner. Specifically:
1. Is the current contract compatible with both strategies?
2. Should the seam interface include a hint (e.g., `reranker.supportsStreaming`) to let implementations signal their strategy?

This is a forward-compatibility decision, not a v0.1 implementation task.
acceptance_criteria: The public-api spec's queryStream() contract explicitly states the rerank ordering guarantee and whether it is forward-compatible with interleaved reranking in v0.3. The reranker seam interface is noted as potentially needing a streaming hint.
close_reason: Spec updated: rerank-all-then-yield is the explicit v0.1 guarantee; v0.3 forward-compatibility path documented via supportsStreaming + streamingRerank opt-in.

title: Decide: Worker offload threshold for query() — at what corpus size?
description:
## Why this decision exists

Brute-force flat SIMD cosine search is O(N) over all stored vectors. For small corpora (< 1K chunks) this is imperceptible on the main thread. For the upper bound of v0.1's target (~50K chunks × 384 dims × int8), the math suggests ~20M operations per query — potentially 20–100 ms on a mid-range device, which causes jank.

SPEC.md defers this to v0.3 ('Worker offload threshold — at what corpus size?'). But the decision must be captured now because:
1. The query() API must signal whether it yields back to the event loop between chunks.
2. If Workers are ever added, the API signature must not be incompatible with what ships in v0.1.

## Current default

Main-thread only for v0.1. Worker offload is v0.3 scope.

## What needs to be decided

1. Confirm v0.1 is synchronous on the main thread (wrapped in async for API uniformity).
2. Record the expected measurement that would trigger the v0.3 worker path, so the benchmark gate isn't arbitrary.
3. Confirm the async Promise API in v0.1 is forward-compatible with a Worker-delegated implementation in v0.3 (no API change needed).
